stem: What does the acronym IP stand for in PRI pelvic nomenclature?
explanation: <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo">IP</abbr> = Ilio-Pubo, describing the anterior pelvic inlet bordered by the ASIS, sacral base, and pubic symphysis. B describes <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo">IsP</abbr> (anterior outlet). C describes <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr> (posterior inlet). D is not a PRI acronym.

stem: What does IP ER describe in tri-planar terms?
explanation: <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> = Ilio-Pubo External Rotation = flexion, abduction, external rotation (opening of the anterior inlet). A describes <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> (closing). C and D mix components incorrectly.

stem: Which five positions are equivalent in the master equivalence chain for the Left AIC patterned position?

options.text: L IP ER = L IS IR = L IsP IR = L SI IR = L AF ER
L IP IR = L IS ER = L IsP ER = L SI ER = L AF IR
L IP ER = L IS ER = L IsP ER = L SI ER = L AF ER
L IP ER = L IS IR = L IsP ER = L SI IR = L AF ER

explanation: The <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> patterned position: inlet and <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr> share <abbr title="External Rotation">ER</abbr>; posterior inlet (<abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr>) and posterior outlet (<abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr>) share <abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr>; anterior outlet (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo">IsP</abbr>) is <abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr> (opposite of inlet). B is the corrected/neutral chain. C and D violate the reciprocal inlet-outlet rule.

stem: The master equivalence rule states that inlet direction matches which other direction?
explanation: Inlet direction matches femur (<abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr>) direction. Outlet direction is always opposite of inlet. C (<abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr>) matches outlet, not inlet. D applies only in <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> (where both sides match), not as a general rule.

stem: If the left anterior inlet is in IP ER, the left anterior outlet is in:
explanation: Inlet and outlet are reciprocal. When the inlet opens (<abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>), the outlet closes (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr>). A violates the reciprocal rule. C and D describe posterior structures, not the anterior outlet.

stem: What does IS IR describe?

options.text: Nutation — ilium rotating inward on the sacrum at the posterior inlet
Acetabulum rotating internally on the femur

explanation: <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr> = Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation = nutation (ilium rotating inward on sacrum). A describes <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> (counter-nutation). C describes <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr>. D describes <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr>.

stem: In PRI nomenclature, when they say "IP ER," the "ER" describes:

options.text: Standard femoral external rotation at the hip joint
An innominate bone motion pattern relative to the pubic symphysis
Lateral rotation of the femoral head in the acetabulum

explanation: PRI's <abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr>/<abbr title="External Rotation">ER</abbr> for <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo">IP</abbr>, <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo">IsP</abbr>, <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr>, and <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> joints describe innominate bone motions, not femoral joint motions, even though the tri-planar descriptors match conventional hip terminology. A and D conflate innominate and femoral motion. C describes <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> motion, not <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo">IP</abbr>.

stem: What is the difference between AF and FA in PRI terminology?

options.text: AF = pelvis moving on fixed femur; FA = femur moving on fixed pelvis
AF = femur moving on pelvis; FA = pelvis moving on femur
AF = anterior femoral; FA = fixed acetabular
They describe the same motion from different sides

explanation: <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr> = Acetabular-Femoral = acetabulum (pelvis) rotating on a fixed femur (closed chain). <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular">FA</abbr> = Femoral-Acetabular = femur moving on a fixed pelvis (open chain). B reverses them. C invents wrong expansions. D is incorrect — they describe different mechanical scenarios.

stem: L AF ER is equivalent to which anterior inlet position?
explanation: L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral External Rotation">AF ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>. Inlet direction matches femur (<abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr>) direction. A is the inverse chain. C is the wrong side. D describes the outlet, which is opposite.

stem: If the right side is in R IP IR, what is the right posterior inlet position?
explanation: R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> is the corrected/closed inlet. In the inverse chain: R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> = R <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = R <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> = R <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac External Rotation">SI ER</abbr> = R <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr>. The posterior inlet (<abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr>) follows the inverse of the anterior inlet's direction designation but the same chain logic. A would pair with <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>. C and D describe outlet, not inlet.

stem: Which acronym describes the posterior pelvic outlet?
explanation: <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> = Sacro-Ilio = posterior outlet (sacrum rotating on ilium). A = <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr> = posterior inlet. B = <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo">IP</abbr> = anterior inlet. D = <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo">IsP</abbr> = anterior outlet.

stem: The pelvic inlet is bordered by which three landmarks?
explanation: The pelvic inlet (superior ring) is bordered by the ASIS, sacral base, and pubic symphysis. A describes the pelvic outlet borders. C and D include non-ring landmarks.

stem: The pelvic outlet is bordered by which three landmarks?
explanation: The pelvic outlet (inferior ring) is bordered by the ischial tuberosities, coccyx, and pubic symphysis. A describes the inlet. C and D mix inlet and outlet landmarks.

stem: What does nutation correspond to in PRI acronym terms?

options.text: IS IR (ilium rotating inward on sacrum)
SI IR (sacrum rotating inward on ilium)

explanation: Nutation = <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr>. The sacral base tips anterior and the ilium rotates inward on the sacrum, compressing the <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> joint. A is the opposite. C describes the posterior outlet. D describes the anterior inlet.

stem: In L AIC, the left SI joint is compressed (nutated). What is the state of the right SI joint?
explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left sacrum is nutated (L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr>, compressed <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr>) while the right is counter-nutated (R <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr>, decompressed <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr>). The sacrotuberous ligament is taut on the left and lax on the right, contributing to right <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> instability complaints. A describes <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. C and D are not consistent with patterned presentations.

stem: What does PRI mean by "anterior glute med"?

options.text: The anterior fibers of the gluteus medius muscle
The proximal iliacus — fibers that adduct the inlet in closed chain

explanation: PRI's "anterior glute med" refers to the proximal iliacus. In closed chain, proximal iliacus pulls the ilium medially (inlet adduction = <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>). PRI labels it this way because it opposes hip abduction at the inlet level. A is the literal reading but incorrect in PRI context. Facilitated in Step 3 of the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> hierarchy.

stem: In the L AIC patterned position, L IP ER on the left side corresponds to what position on the right?
explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left pelvis is anteriorly tilted (L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>) while the right pelvis is the compensatory opposite (R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> — closed inlet). The manual states: L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> = R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>. A would be <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. C describes the left outlet in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, not the right inlet. D describes the left, not right, posterior inlet in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>.

stem: In B PEC, what is the bilateral inlet position?

options.text: Bilateral IP ER (both inlets open — bilateral anterior tilt)
Left IP ER, Right IP IR

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> = bilateral anterior pelvic tilt = both inlets in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>. Both sides look like the left side in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>. A is the corrected position. C describes <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> asymmetry. D describes the outlet, which is opposite — both outlets are in <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr>.

stem: In B PEC, what is the bilateral outlet position?

options.text: Left IsP IR, Right IsP ER

explanation: In <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>, both inlets are in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> (open) so both outlets are reciprocally in <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr> (closed). Both pelvic diaphragms are descended. A is the corrected outlet position. C describes <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> asymmetry. D is the posterior outlet corrected position.

stem: What is the corrected left pelvic position in PRI equivalence terms?

options.text: L IP ER = L IS IR = L IsP IR = L SI IR = L AF ER
L IP IR = L IS ER = L IsP ER = L SI ER = L AF IR
L IP IR = L IS IR = L IsP IR = L SI IR = L AF IR
L IP ER = L IS ER = L IsP ER = L SI ER = L AF ER

explanation: The corrected/neutral chain is the inverse of the patterned chain: L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac External Rotation">SI ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr>. Inlet closes (<abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>), outlet opens (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr>), counter-nutation (<abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr>). A is the patterned <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> position. C and D violate reciprocal rules.

stem: How is the [[ADT]] performed?

options.text: Patient supine, hip flexed 90°, knee flexed 90°. Examiner passively adducts the femur. Positive = femur won't adduct past midline.
Patient sidelying, both hips/knees 90°. Patient actively drops the top knee toward the mat.
Patient sidelying, top leg straight. Examiner passively abducts the top leg.
Patient standing, reaching toward toes while shifting one hip back.

explanation: [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] = Adduction Drop Test. Patient supine, hip and knee flexed 90°, examiner passively adducts the femur. Positive = femur won't adduct cleanly in the acetabulum given pelvic orientation. B describes [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]]. C describes [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]]. D describes [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]].

stem: How is the [[PADT]] performed?

options.text: Patient supine, examiner passively adducts the femur
Patient sidelying, both hips/knees 90°; patient actively brings the top knee toward the mat while maintaining 90° knee flexion
Patient standing, forward bending to touch toes
Patient sidelying, top leg straight; examiner passively abducts the top leg

explanation: [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] = Pelvic Ascension Drop Test. Patient sidelying, both hips/knees at 90°. The patient actively drops the top knee toward the mat. Positive = knee can't reach mat (pelvic diaphragm can't ascend). A describes [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]]. C describes [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]]. D describes [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]].

stem: What does a positive [[PADT]] indicate?

options.text: The ipsilateral pelvic diaphragm cannot ascend because the outlet ischium can't abduct and the inlet ilium can't adduct
Limited femoral abduction due to lateral acetabular buttressing

explanation: Positive [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] = the pelvic diaphragm on that side cannot ascend. The outlet ischium can't abduct and the inlet ilium can't adduct. Cause: overactivity of distal iliacus interfering with proximal iliacus's ability to adduct the inlet. B is a generic finding, not PRI-specific. C describes positive [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]]. D is unrelated.

stem: How is the [[PART]] performed?

options.text: Patient sidelying, bottom knee/hip flexed 70–90°, top leg straight; examiner passively abducts the top leg while stabilizing the innominate
Patient supine, hip flexed 90°; examiner passively adducts the femur
Patient sidelying, both hips/knees 90°; patient actively drops top knee
Patient standing, reaching forward with hip shift

explanation: [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] = Passive Abduction Raise Test. Patient sidelying, bottom hip/knee flexed, top leg straight. Examiner passively abducts the top leg, stabilizing the innominate, stopping if thoracic sidebending occurs. Positive = abduction < 45°. B describes [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]]. C describes [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]]. D describes [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]].

stem: In the L AIC pattern, which side is the [[PART]] expected to be positive?

options.text: Neither — [[PART]] is negative in L AIC

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] is positive on the right. The right inlet is in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> (closed) — the compensatory position causes lateral buttressing of the right femoral head against the superior acetabulum, limiting right hip abduction. Left [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] and left [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] are positive, but [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] is positive on the opposite side.

stem: In PRI testing, what does "positive" mean?

options.text: The pathology has been provoked (as in standard orthopedic special tests)
The patient cannot achieve the desired position or movement
The patient has normal range of motion
The test is inconclusive and must be repeated

explanation: In PRI, positive = the patient cannot do it. This is inverted from standard orthopedic testing where a positive test means pathology was provoked. Positive [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] = femur won't adduct. Positive [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] = knee can't reach mat. Positive [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] = abduction < 45°.

stem: How is the [[SRT]] performed differently for L AIC versus B PEC?

options.text: L AIC: reach toward left toes with left hip shift back; B PEC: reach straight down, hips move back symmetrically
L AIC: reach straight down; B PEC: reach with hip shift
L AIC uses a seated version; B PEC uses standing

explanation: For <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the patient reaches toward left toes with both hands while shifting the left hip back, then repeats toward the right. For <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> (no hip shift), the patient reaches straight down toward the floor with hips moving back symmetrically. Positive = > 0 inches from floor.

stem: What is the expected test profile for the L AIC pattern?
explanation: <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>: [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] and [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] positive on the left, [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] positive on the right (note the asymmetry). [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]] > 0 inches, Squat < 3/5, [[<abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr>]] < 3/5. B is <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. C is <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. D incorrectly puts [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] on the left.

stem: What is the expected test profile for B PEC?
explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>: bilateral positive [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] and [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]], bilateral negative [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]], [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]] > 0" (can't touch toes), Squat < 3/5, [[<abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr>]] < 3/5. A is <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. C is <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>. D mixes <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> and Patho features.

stem: Which three tests distinguish B Patho PEC from standard B PEC?
explanation: The three distinguishing tests: (1) [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]]: negative bilateral in <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>, positive in Patho. (2) [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]]: can't touch toes in <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>, CAN touch toes in Patho. (3) Squat: < 3/5 in <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>, > 3/5 in Patho. [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]], [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]], and [[<abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr>]] are the same in both patterns.

stem: A patient has +B [[ADT]], +B [[PADT]], +B [[PART]], [[SRT]] = 0 inches, Squat 4/5, [[HALT]] 1/5. Most consistent with:
explanation: Bilateral positive [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] and [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] with positive [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]], <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> = 0 (touches toes), and Squat > 3/5 = <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. The paradoxical performance — better ROM/squat = worse pathology. Compensatory hypermobility, not motor control. <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> would have negative [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]], <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> > 0, Squat < 3/5.

stem: A patient scores 4/5 on the Functional Squat with bilateral positive [[ADT]] and [[PADT]]. What does this suggest?

options.text: Good motor control — the patient is improving
Compensatory hypermobility consistent with B Patho PEC
Isolated ankle mobility — unrelated to the pelvis

explanation: High squat score (> 3/5) with positive [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]]/[[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] = Patho <abbr title="Posterior Exterior Chain">PEC</abbr>. This is paradoxical performance: better ROM does not mean better function. It indicates compensatory hypermobility driven by long and tonic muscles. D is wrong because <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> shows Squat < 3/5.

stem: What does the Functional Squat test assess at Level 1?

options.text: Ability to initiate a squat (slight knee bend); inability indicates hyperactive extensors, rectus femoris, sartorius
Ability to get bottom below knees with heels down
Ability to achieve maximal squat with COG through heels

explanation: Squat Level 1 = ability to initiate the squat with a slight knee bend and trunk flexion. Inability = hyperactive extensors, rectus femoris, and sartorius preventing the initiation of flexion. B describes Level 3. C describes Level 5. D is not a squat level.

stem: A patient cannot get their bottom back during a squat — knees shoot forward and trunk flexes excessively. This failure corresponds to Functional Squat Level:
explanation: Level 2: can't get bottom back, knees forward, trunk flexed. Hyperactive hip flexors and <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular">FA</abbr> external rotators with lack of femoral adduction. Level 1 = can't even initiate. Level 3 = can't get below knees. Level 4 = can't achieve full squat.

stem: Squat Level 3 inability indicates which hyperactive muscles?

options.text: Hip flexors and FA external rotators
Intercostals, tibialis anterior/posterior, with poor IO/TA integration

explanation: Level 3 inability (can't get bottom below knees, heels down, trunk flexed) = hyperactive intercostals, tib ant/post, and poor <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/<abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> integration. A = Level 1. B = Level 2. D = Level 4.

stem: Squat Level 5 inability reflects:

options.text: Lack of maximal AF IR and poor respiratory/pelvic diaphragm synchronization

explanation: Level 5 = maximal squat with COG through heels. Inability = lack of maximal <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> and poor synchronization between the respiratory and pelvic diaphragms. A = Level 4. B = Level 4. D = Level 1.

stem: What is the patient position for the [[HALT]]?

options.text: Supine, hips/knees flexed 90°, feet on wall
Sidelying, top leg extended aligned with hip/shoulder, bottom leg flexed with toes on wall on a bolster
Standing on one leg, reaching forward

explanation: [[<abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr>]] setup: patient sidelying, top leg extended aligned with hip/shoulder with top knee above shoulder level. Bottom leg flexed with toes on wall on a 4–6 inch bolster. Top palm flat in front of chest, bottom hand under head. A describes the 90-90 position. C and D are not <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> positions.

stem: A patient fails [[HALT]] at Level 0. What does this mean?

options.text: IO/TA weakness with inability to inhibit inlet abduction of the flexed leg
Can't position (malaligned pelvis) and can't inhibit outlet abduction of the extended leg — reposition first
Poor IC adductor/anterior glute med integration
Can't extend with glute max during concomitant abduction

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 0 failure = can't even get into position. The pelvis is malaligned and the patient can't inhibit outlet abduction of the extended leg. Facilitation target: repositioning first — pelvis not yet neutral. A = Level 1. C = Level 2. D = Level 5.

stem: A patient fails [[HALT]] at Level 2. What should be facilitated?

options.text: IC adductor and anterior glute med
Glute min and anterior glute med
Contralateral adductor and ipsilateral glute med

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 2 failure = poor IC adductor/anterior glute med with inability to achieve outlet abduction of the flexed leg. Facilitate IC adductor + anterior glute med. A = Level 1. C = Level 3. D = Level 4.

stem: A patient fails [[HALT]] at Level 3. This reflects poor function of which muscles?

options.text: IC adductor and anterior glute med
Glute min and anterior glute med, or labral impingement

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 3 failure = poor glute min/anterior glute med or labral impingement with inability to achieve inlet adduction of the flexed leg. Facilitate glute min + anterior glute med. A = Level 2. B = Level 1. D = Level 5.

stem: [[HALT]] Level 5 failure reflects inability to:

options.text: Inhibit outlet abduction of the extended leg
Achieve inlet adduction of the flexed leg
Extend the leg with glute max during concomitant abduction
Press the bottom toes into the wall

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 5 failure = can't extend with glute max during concomitant abduction and can't achieve outlet adduction of the extended leg. Facilitate glute max extension during abduction. A = Level 0. B = Level 3. D is a setup instruction.

stem: Which five steps does the [[HALT]] progress through?

options.text: 1. Press bottom toes into wall, 2. Press bottom hip into surface, 3. Turn/raise bottom knee, 4. Turn top heel up/toes down, 5. Raise top leg and extend hip
1. Raise top leg, 2. Extend top hip, 3. Rotate trunk, 4. Press into wall, 5. Hold
1. Squeeze ball, 2. Bridge, 3. Extend leg, 4. Rotate, 5. Lower
1. Inhale, 2. Exhale with balloon, 3. Pelvic tilt, 4. Hemibridge, 5. Lower

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> steps: (1) Press bottom toes into wall (stabilize pelvis), (2) Press bottom hip into surface (engage lower abs/adductors), (3) Turn/raise bottom knee using IC adductor and anterior glute med, (4) Turn top heel up/top toes down, (5) Raise top leg off wall and extend hip. Discontinue at failure.

stem: The Apical Expansion Test is positive in L AIC when the patient:

options.text: Can't expand the right anterior chest wall on inhalation
Can't expand the left anterior chest wall on inhalation
Can't expand either anterior chest wall

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the patient can't expand the right anterior chest wall. The right anterior outlet is in abduction (pelvic diaphragm ascended on right), and the right anterior inlet is in extension. B would be the opposite finding. C describes <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. D is not a standard PRI finding.

stem: The Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test is positive in L AIC when the patient:

options.text: Can't fill the right posterior mediastinum
Can't fill the left posterior mediastinum in right sidelying

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the patient can't fill the left posterior mediastinum in right sidelying. The left anterior outlet is in adduction, pelvic diaphragm descended, left inlet in flexion — air is pushed into the left anterior outlet/chest wall instead of the posterior mediastinum. C describes <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>.

stem: Which functional relationship chain connects pelvic and respiratory test findings?

options.text: +L [[PADT]] → +L Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test → +R Apical Expansion Test
+[[SRT]] → +L Apical Expansion Test → +R Posterior Mediastinum Test

explanation: The functional relationship chain: positive L [[<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>]] predicts positive L Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test, which predicts positive R Apical Expansion Test. B, C, and D misconnect the test relationships.

stem: A positive left [[ADT]] most directly indicates limitation in:

options.text: Left posterior inlet IR — the left femur cannot adduct in the acetabulum given the pelvic orientation
Left anterior outlet ER — the left outlet cannot open
Right posterior inlet IR — the right sacrum can't nutate
Right anterior inlet ER — the right inlet can't open

explanation: Positive L [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] indicates the left femur won't adduct cleanly in the acetabulum. The left inlet is in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> (open), and the acetabular orientation blocks femoral adduction. B mixes outlet with <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>. C and D describe the wrong side.

stem: When [[ADT]] is negative bilaterally but right [[PART]] remains positive, the cause shifts to:

options.text: Lateral acetabular buttressing from L AIC compensatory position
Right proximal iliacus overactivity blocking right glute max — facilitate right glute max

explanation: When [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] is negative (pelvis repositioned) but R [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]] persists, the cause is no longer acetabular buttressing from pelvic malalignment. It shifts to right proximal iliacus overactivity blocking right glute max. Facilitate right glute max. A applies only when <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> is still positive.

stem: After B PEC repositioning, test results are: −B [[ADT]], +L [[PADT]], −R [[PADT]], −L [[PART]], +R [[PART]]. The next step is:

options.text: Promote left inlet adduction via left proximal iliacus

explanation: In the <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> case study, visit 3 shows: −B <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>, +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>/−R <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, −L <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>/+R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>. The asymmetry indicates the left side needs further work — promote left inlet adduction via left proximal iliacus. C would apply only if all tests were negative. B is not needed since <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> cleared.

stem: What does the [[SRT]] most broadly assess?

options.text: Global stacking, respiratory mechanics, and inlet/outlet ON-OFF states

explanation: The [[<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>]] is a global measure reflecting stacking, respiratory mechanics, and inlet/outlet ON-OFF states. It is not an isolated tissue-length test. A–D each describe only one possible contributor.

stem: In L AIC, a positive left [[ADT]] predicts a positive [[PART]] on which side?
explanation: Positive left [[<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>]] predicts positive right [[<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>]]. The left inlet is stuck in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> (blocks left femoral adduction → +L <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>), while the right side's compensatory position (R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>) blocks right femoral abduction → +R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>.

stem: Which muscles belong to the Brown (Sagittal Plane) family in Handout 1?

options.text: R rectus femoris (inlet), R sartorius (inlet), L hamstring (outlet), L pubococcygeus (outlet), L puborectalis (outlet)
R gluteus maximus (inlet & outlet), R piriformis (outlet), R coccygeus (outlet)

explanation: Brown (Sagittal) = R rectus femoris (inlet), R sartorius (inlet), L hamstring (outlet), L pubococcygeus (outlet), L puborectalis (outlet). B = Red (Adduction) + Orange (Transverse). C = Green (Abduction). D = Violet (<abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr>).

stem: The left iliacus belongs to which color family?
explanation: Left iliacus = Red family (Frontal Plane Adduction) — it acts at the inlet. In closed chain, proximal iliacus adducts the left inlet (<abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>). A = sagittal plane muscles. C = outlet abduction muscles. D = internal rotation muscles.

stem: Which muscles belong to the Green (Frontal Plane Abduction) family?
explanation: Green (Abduction) = L obturator internus (outlet) and L iliococcygeus (outlet). These muscles abduct the outlet — pulling the ischial tuberosities apart. B = Violet. C = Yellow. D = Brown.

stem: The Yellow (Integration) family includes:

options.text: R gluteus maximus (inlet & outlet), R piriformis (outlet)
L IO (inlet), L TA (inlet), L arcuate tendon (outlet), L anococcygeal ligament (outlet)

explanation: Yellow (Integration) = L <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr> (inlet), L <abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> (inlet), L arcuate tendon (outlet), L anococcygeal ligament (outlet). These connect the respiratory diaphragm to pelvic inlet control. A = Orange. B = Violet. D = Brown.

stem: The right gluteus maximus works at which regions in PRI?

options.text: Neither — it is out of scope for pelvic restoration

explanation: R gluteus maximus works at both the inlet (superior fibers producing <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> / inlet abduction) and the outlet (inferior fibers producing <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac Internal Rotation">SI IR</abbr> / outlet closure). It belongs to the Violet (<abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr>) family and is facilitated in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> repositioning and again in Step 4.

stem: What does the left obturator internus do in PRI's closed-chain framework?

options.text: Lateral rotation of the femur (standard open-chain action)
Pulls the left ischial tuberosity laterally, widening the left outlet (IsP ER)
Flexes the hip in open chain

explanation: In closed chain, the left obturator internus pulls the ischium laterally instead of rotating the femur. This widens the left outlet (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> = outlet abduction). A is the standard open-chain action. C is incorrect for <abbr title="Obturator Internus">OI</abbr>. D describes the proximal iliacus.

stem: Which muscles govern the pelvic inlet (anterior)?
explanation: The anterior pelvic inlet is governed by iliacus, rectus femoris, and sartorius, with <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs connecting the respiratory diaphragm to inlet control. A describes the anterior outlet. C describes the posterior outlet. D mixes inlet and outlet muscles.

stem: Which muscles govern the pelvic outlet (anterior)?
explanation: The anterior pelvic outlet is governed by obturator internus, iliococcygeus, puborectalis, and pubococcygeus. These pelvic floor muscles control outlet abduction (opening) and adduction (closing). A = anterior inlet. C = posterior inlet. D = integration/inlet.

stem: Which muscles govern the posterior pelvic outlet?
explanation: The posterior pelvic outlet is governed by piriformis, coccygeus, and glute max. B describes the posterior inlet. C = anterior inlet. D = anterior outlet.

stem: What is the standard anatomical action of the iliacus?
explanation: Standard action: hip flexion (joining with psoas at the lesser trochanter). PRI splits iliacus into distal (flexor, part of <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> problem) and proximal (inlet adduction in closed chain — the corrective action). B = glute max. C = glute med. D = anterior glute med fibers.

stem: PRI distinguishes proximal vs. distal iliacus. What does the proximal iliacus do in closed chain?

options.text: Flexes the hip by pulling the femur upward
Pulls the ilium medially — inlet adduction (IP IR)

explanation: Proximal iliacus in closed chain pulls the ilium medially = inlet adduction = <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>. This is facilitated in Step 3. PRI calls this muscle "anterior glute med." A is the distal iliacus action (part of the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> problem). C and D are not iliacus actions.

stem: What is the origin and insertion of the gluteus maximus?

options.text: Origin: iliac fossa. Insertion: lesser trochanter
Origin: posterior gluteal line, dorsal sacrum/coccyx, thoracolumbar fascia, sacrotuberous ligament. Insertion: IT band and gluteal tuberosity
Origin: outer ilium between gluteal lines. Insertion: lateral greater trochanter
Origin: obturator membrane. Insertion: medial greater trochanter

explanation: Glute max: origin from posterior gluteal line, dorsal sacrum/coccyx, thoracolumbar fascia, sacrotuberous ligament; insertion into IT band and gluteal tuberosity. A = iliacus. C = gluteus medius. D = obturator internus.

stem: In L AIC, which muscles are part of the Anterior Interior Chain?

options.text: Diaphragm → psoas → iliacus → TFL → vastus lateralis → biceps femoris

explanation: The <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr> = Anterior Interior Chain: diaphragm → psoas → iliacus → <abbr title="Tensor Fasciae Latae">TFL</abbr> → vastus lateralis → biceps femoris (left side). B = posterior outlet muscles. C = integration muscles. D mixes inlet muscles from different sides.

stem: The left IO/TAs connect which two structures in PRI's framework?

options.text: The left respiratory diaphragm to the left pelvic inlet
The left outlet to the right outlet

explanation: Left <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs are the primary connectors linking the left respiratory diaphragm to left pelvic inlet control — the "conduit for airflow and pressure regulation" between the two diaphragms. They promote inlet extension (<abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>) and regulate intra-abdominal pressure.

stem: What is the Zone of Apposition ([[ZOA]])?

options.text: The area of the diaphragm that apposes the inner ribcage — its dome shape allows efficient contraction
The space between the two innominates
The contact surface between the femoral head and acetabulum
The overlap zone between the IO and TA muscles

explanation: <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr> = the curved area where the diaphragm muscle apposes the inner ribcage. When the diaphragm is domed (good <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr>), it contracts efficiently. In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left diaphragm loses <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr> — becomes flat, postural, and inefficient. B–D are not <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr>.

stem: In L AIC, why does the left hemidiaphragm lose its ZOA?

options.text: The left hemidiaphragm lacks liver support underneath, causing it to flatten
The left lung is larger than the right
The left psoas is too short to allow diaphragm descent
The right hemidiaphragm compresses the left

explanation: The left hemidiaphragm lacks the liver's structural support underneath (the liver sits under the right hemidiaphragm). Without that support, the left diaphragm flattens, loses its dome, and becomes a postural muscle rather than a respiratory one. B is backwards — the left lung is smaller. C and D are not accurate.

stem: In L AIC, what happens to the left IO/TAs?

options.text: They become weak — the left abdominal wall can't oppose the left psoas/iliacus pull
They hypertrophy to compensate for the pattern

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs become weak. The left abdominal wall can't oppose the leftward pull of the psoas/iliacus, contributing to the anterior tilt and forward rotation. Restoring <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/<abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> function is essential for maintaining repositioning. In <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>, bilateral <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs are long and weak.

stem: Which muscle's role as a femoral rotator is debated in standard kinesiology, though PRI makes position-specific claims about it?
explanation: The psoas's role as a femoral rotator is debated and context-dependent in standard kinesiology. PRI claims it is an "ineffective femoral external rotator" — this is position-specific. The broader point that the psoas becomes positionally shortened and disadvantaged in anterior tilt is valid.

stem: In L AIC, the compensatory right-side position causes overactivity of which muscles?

options.text: Right IO/TAs and right glute med
Right vastus lateralis and right biceps femoris
Right obturator internus and right iliococcygeus

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left femur orients inward via the acetabulum, causing compensatory overactivity of the vastus lateralis and biceps femoris as antagonistic hip stabilizers. A would be corrective muscles. C = pelvic floor muscles. D = left-side problem muscles.

stem: What is the left adductor magnus's role in L AIC correction?

options.text: It directly repositions the pelvis posteriorly
It facilitates left AF IR and activates the left pelvic floor muscles for outlet abduction

explanation: Left adductor magnus facilitates left <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> (pelvis rotates posteriorly on the left femur) and activates the left pelvic floor muscles (obturator internus, iliococcygeus) for outlet abduction. Used in the Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back exercise. C is not an adductor action. D is opposite of the corrective direction.

stem: Which pattern is most associated with pubalgia?
explanation: Pubalgia is associated with <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. Bilateral <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> creates eccentrically lengthened rectus abdominis and short paraspinals. Bilateral <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr> creates lengthened adductors with shearing at the pubic symphysis. A is asymmetric. C is also bilateral but with different mechanism. D is upper quadrant.

stem: Levator ani syndrome that is "typically more prominent on the left side" is most consistent with:
explanation: Levator ani syndrome with left-side predominance is consistent with <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>. The left pelvic diaphragm is descended in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> (L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr>). The PRI manual states this condition is "typically more prominent on the left side." A and C are bilateral. D is not a standard PRI pattern.

stem: A patient has right-sided SI joint instability pain. Which pattern best explains this?

options.text: L AIC — right SI is counter-nutated and decompressed
B Patho PEC — bilateral SI laxity
L AIC — left SI is decompressed

explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> is compressed (L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr> = nutation) while the right <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> is distracted (R <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = counter-nutation). Right <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> decompression = instability complaints on the right. The sacrotuberous and sacrospinous ligaments are slack on the right. D gets the side wrong.

stem: Coccygodynia is most associated with:

options.text: B PEC or B Patho PEC

explanation: Coccygodynia is associated with <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> or <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. Bilateral outlet is in <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr> (closed), coccyx is flexed anteriorly, coccygeus is hypertonic. This creates pelvic floor dysfunction at the posterior outlet. A is asymmetric. C is upper quadrant.

stem: A patient with bilateral positive [[ADT]]/[[PADT]] who can touch their toes easily and squat 4/5 should be suspected of:
explanation: Touching toes (<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> = 0) with positive bilateral <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>/<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> and Squat > 3/5 = <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. Better ROM = worse pathology. This is compensatory hypermobility driven by long and tonic muscles, not motor control. <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> can't touch toes and has Squat < 3/5.

stem: Stress urinary incontinence is most associated with which pattern?

options.text: B PEC or B Patho PEC

explanation: SUI is associated with <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>/<abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>. The bilateral pelvic diaphragm is descended, hypertonic, and inflexible (<abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>) or descended and bulged with high resting tone (Patho). The pelvic floor has lost the ability to cycle between descent and ascension.

stem: In B PEC, the respiratory findings include:

options.text: Unilateral left rib flare with left anterior chest hyperinflation
Bilateral anterior rib flare, bilateral diaphragms descended, bilateral IO/TAs long/weak, continual state of inhalation
Bilateral posterior rib flare with bilateral posterior chest hyperinflation

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> respiratory findings: bilateral anterior ribs flared/<abbr title="External Rotation">ER</abbr>, bilateral diaphragms descended/linear/postural (poor bilateral <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr>), bilateral <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs long/weak, continual state of inhalation with passive exhalation. A describes <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> (unilateral). C and D are not PRI pattern findings.

stem: What differentiates B PEC from B Patho PEC pelvic diaphragm findings?

options.text: B PEC: pelvic diaphragm descended/hypertonic/inflexible. Patho: descended/bulged with high resting tone and abdominal/pelvic bulge
B PEC: pelvic diaphragm ascended. Patho: pelvic diaphragm descended
B PEC: bilateral pelvic bulge. Patho: no pelvic bulge

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>: pelvic diaphragm is descended, hypertonic, and inflexible (but not bulged). <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>: all <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> findings PLUS abdominal AND pelvic bulge, rectus abdominis eccentrically tight/strained, pelvic diaphragm descended/bulged with high resting tone. D reverses them.

stem: If a patient's Functional Squat is > 3/5 (Patho), what modification should be made to balloon exercises?

options.text: Patient likely cannot handle blowing up a balloon — use a straw, kazoo, or party favor instead
Add a resistance band to the balloon

explanation: If Squat > 3/5 (Patho), the patient likely cannot handle blowing up a balloon. The manual notes this twice. Use a straw, kazoo, or party favor instead. The increased <abbr title="Intra-Abdominal Pressure">IAP</abbr> from balloon inflation may overwhelm the already-compromised pelvic floor.

stem: PRI's A-B-A clinical design means:

options.text: A: baseline testing, B: intervention + retest, second A: withdrawal + retest. If tests revert during second A, the intervention caused the change.
A: assessment, B: bilateral treatment, A: asymmetric treatment
A: anterior tilt correction, B: bilateral correction, A: assessment
A: randomize, B: blind, A: control

explanation: A-B-A = single-subject experimental design. First A = baseline testing. B = intervention then retest. Second A = withdrawal, retest. If tests revert, the intervention caused the change. Each patient is their own control. Strong internal validity.

stem: What is the correct treatment sequence in PRI?
explanation: Reposition → Inhibit → Facilitate. First move the pelvis out of its patterned position, then quiet overactive muscles, then activate underactive muscles. Skipping repositioning means facilitation targets muscles that can't fire correctly from a malaligned position.

stem: What are the three L AIC repositioning techniques?

options.text: Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift
90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, Left Sidelying Right Glute Max
Wall Short Seated Left Arm Reach, Squatting Bar Reverse Reach, All Four Belly Lift
Standing Wall Supported Resisted Reach, 90-90 Respiratory Left Hip Shift, Seated Bar Reach

explanation: <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> repositioning = (1) 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, (2) Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, (3) Left Sidelying Right Glute Max. A = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>/Patho repositioning. C and D are facilitation/inhibition exercises used later.

stem: What are the three B PEC / B Patho PEC repositioning techniques?

options.text: 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, Left Sidelying Right Glute Max
Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift
90-90 Respiratory Left Hip Shift, Standing Wall Supported Resisted Reach, Squatting Bar Reverse Reach
Wall Short Seated Left Arm Reach, Supine Hemi Extension, All Four Right Glute Max

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> and <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> use the SAME three repositioning techniques: (1) Standing Wall Supported Reach, (2) 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular Internal Rotation">FA IR</abbr> with Balloon, (3) Modified All Four Belly Lift. These are bilateral/symmetrical. A = <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> repositioning. C and D are later-stage exercises.

stem: After L AIC repositioning, tests show: −L [[ADT]], +L [[PADT]], +R [[PART]]. The patient should follow:
explanation: −L <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> with +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> ± R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> = proceed to Pelvis Restoration facilitation (<abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> hierarchy, maximize hemi 90-90 position). A applies only when all tests are negative. C applies when <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> doesn't clear. D is upper quadrant.

stem: After L AIC repositioning, tests show: −L [[ADT]], −L [[PADT]], −R [[PART]]. The patient should follow:
explanation: All tests negative after repositioning = the pelvis is neutral and the facilitation hierarchy is not needed. Transition to Myokinematic Restoration (a different PRI course). A is only needed if <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>/<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> persist. D is unnecessary since tests cleared.

stem: After B PEC repositioning, tests show: −B [[ADT]], +B [[PADT]]. The next step is:

options.text: Pelvis Restoration program (maximize inlet extension via IO/TAs, forward arm reach, proximal adductors)

explanation: −B <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> with +B <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> = proceed to Pelvis Restoration (maximize inlet extension via <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs, forward arm reach, proximal adductors). B applies when both <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> and <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> are negative. C applies when <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> doesn't clear. D is a different pattern.

stem: After B PEC repositioning, tests show: −B [[ADT]], −B [[PADT]]. The next step is:
explanation: −B <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> and −B <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> = pelvis neutral. Transition to Myokinematic Restoration & Postural Respiration program. A is only needed when <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists.

stem: After repositioning, if all tests remain positive, the clinician should:

options.text: Re-evaluate repositioning technique or look at assessment tests from other PRI courses
Switch to B Patho PEC treatment regardless of pattern

explanation: If all tests remain positive after repositioning, further assessment is needed. Re-evaluate the repositioning techniques for correct performance, or look at assessment tests from other PRI courses. Do not skip to facilitation — you can't facilitate what's not repositioned.

stem: Step 1 of the L AIC facilitation hierarchy targets which muscles?

options.text: Left puborectalis, left pubococcygeus, left iliococcygeus, left obturator internus
Right rectus femoris and right sartorius
Left proximal iliacus and left gluteus medius
Right coccygeus, right piriformis, right glute max

explanation: Step 1: Turn ON right anterior inlet (<abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>) via right rectus femoris and right sartorius. Test markers: +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, +R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, R <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 0/5. Exercise: 90-90 Supported Left Hip Shift with Right Rectus Femoris and Sartorius. A = Step 2. C = Step 3. D = Step 4.

stem: Step 2 of the L AIC facilitation hierarchy targets which muscles?

options.text: Right rectus femoris and right sartorius
Left puborectalis, left pubococcygeus, left iliococcygeus, left obturator internus (via left adductors)
Left proximal iliacus and left gluteus medius
Right coccygeus, right piriformis, right glute max

explanation: Step 2: Turn ON left anterior outlet (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr>) — pelvic diaphragm ascension. Left adductors are the gateway to activating left pelvic floor muscles. Test markers: +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, +R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, R <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 1/5. A = Step 1. C = Step 3. D = Step 4.

stem: At which step of the L AIC facilitation hierarchy does the right [[PART]] resolve (become negative)?
explanation: R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> resolves at Step 3. Step 3 targets left proximal iliacus + left glute med for left posterior inlet correction (<abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> / counter-nutation). Test markers at Step 3: +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists, −R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> achieved, R <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 2/5. This is a key milestone.

stem: At which step of the L AIC facilitation hierarchy does the left [[PADT]] resolve?
explanation: L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> resolves at Step 4 (becomes negative). Step 4 targets right posterior outlet (<abbr title="Sacro-Iliac Internal Rotation">SI IR</abbr>) via right coccygeus, piriformis, and glute max. After Step 4: both <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> and <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> are negative. Test markers: −L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, −R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, R <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 4/5.

stem: Step 3 of the L AIC hierarchy facilitates left posterior inlet correction. What is the PRI position being targeted?

options.text: L IS ER (counter-nutation — ilium rotating outward on sacrum)

explanation: Step 3 targets L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = counter-nutation of the left sacrum. Left proximal iliacus adducts the left inlet while left glute med produces left <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr>. This corrects the left ilium's nutation on the sacrum. A is the patterned inlet position. C = Step 2 target. D = Step 4 target.

stem: Step 4 of the L AIC hierarchy targets which position?

options.text: Right posterior outlet closing (R SI IR)
Left anterior inlet opening (L IP ER)
Right anterior inlet opening (R IP ER)
Left anterior outlet opening (L IsP ER)

explanation: Step 4: Turn ON right posterior outlet via R <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac Internal Rotation">SI IR</abbr> — right coccygeus, right piriformis, right glute max. The right posterior outlet closes via right glute max extension with <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular External Rotation">FA ER</abbr> and <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular">FA</abbr> abduction. B = patterned position. C = Step 1. D = Step 2.

stem: What exercise is associated with Step 1 of the L AIC hierarchy?

options.text: Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back with Balloon
90-90 Supported Left Hip Shift with Right Rectus Femoris and Sartorius
Standing Respiratory Left FA IR with Resisted Left Arm Pull Down

explanation: Step 1 exercise: 90-90 Supported Left Hip Shift with Right Rectus Femoris and Sartorius. The right knee advances ahead of the left, initiating the Right <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr> position. A = Step 2 exercises. C = Step 3 exercises. D = Step 4 exercises.

stem: What exercise is associated with Step 2 of the L AIC hierarchy?

options.text: 90-90 Supported Left Hip Shift with Right Rectus Femoris
Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back with Balloon
Left Stance Interrupted Reciprocal Step Through

explanation: Step 2 exercises include Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back (with Balloon) and Left Sidelying Knee to Knee. The left adductors are the gateway — their contraction facilitates left pelvic floor muscles. A = Step 1. C = Step 4. D = Step 9.

stem: After Step 4 of the L AIC hierarchy, what does the patient progress to?

options.text: Upright functional integration — single-leg control, sidelying integration, reciprocal alternating activity

explanation: After Step 4 (−<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, −<abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 4/5), the patient progresses to upright functional control: Steps 5–10 include single-leg frontal plane control, sidelying integration, seated pelvic ascension control, reciprocal alternating activity, and squatting.

stem: Steps 5–10 of the L AIC hierarchy focus on:

options.text: Integrating the corrected pelvic position into functional movement patterns

explanation: Steps 5–10 integrate the corrected position into real-world movement: single-leg stance, weight shifting, walking, and squatting. Step 5 = left single-leg frontal plane control. Step 9 = reciprocal alternating activity / gait. Step 10 = squatting.

stem: In the 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, the patient should feel engagement of:

options.text: The muscles behind the left thigh (left hamstring)

explanation: In the 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, the patient maintains a pelvic tilt with the left leg on the wall and straightens the right leg. They should feel the muscles behind the left thigh (left hamstring) engage. The left hamstring pulls the left hemipelvis posteriorly.

stem: In the Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, what should the patient feel?

options.text: Left inner thigh (adductor) engaging as the left leg pulls back

explanation: The patient lies on the right side, pulls the left leg back on inhalation, then squeezes the left knee down into a towel on exhalation. They should feel the left inner thigh engage. Left adductor contraction facilitates left <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> and activates the left pelvic floor.

stem: In the Left Sidelying Right Glute Max exercise, the patient should feel:

options.text: Left outer hip (buttock) stretch and right outer hip engagement

explanation: Patient lies on left side. They shift the right hip forward until a stretch is felt in the left outside hip (buttock), then turn the right knee out. They should feel the right outside hip (glute max) engage. R glute max rotates the sacrum back toward the left.

stem: The Standing Wall Supported Reach (B PEC repositioning) targets which primary goal?

options.text: Bilateral anterior inlet and bilateral posterior outlet inhibition (promote inlet extension)

explanation: The Standing Wall Supported Reach targets bilateral anterior inlet and posterior outlet inhibition, promoting inlet extension. The patient squeezes a ball between the knees, rounds the back with lower back flat on wall, and reaches forward on exhale. B, C, D are <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>-specific targets.

stem: In the B Patho PEC hierarchy, what is the first priority after repositioning achieves −B [[ADT]], +B [[PADT]]?

options.text: Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet and bilateral posterior outlet
Begin L AIC alternating program immediately

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> hierarchy (after −B <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>, +B <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>): Step 1 is maximize diaphragmatic respiratory function. This includes 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular Internal Rotation">FA IR</abbr> with Balloon (or straw/kazoo for Patho), Supine Hooklying Synchronized Resisted Glute Max, etc. A is Step 2. C is Step 3.

stem: In the B PEC hierarchy, what follows maximizing diaphragmatic respiratory function?

options.text: Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet and bilateral posterior outlet

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> hierarchy: (1) Maximize diaphragmatic respiratory function, (2) Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet and bilateral posterior outlet (promote inlet extension), (3) Left <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr> alternating program. A is Step 3, not Step 2.

stem: A retest after a repositioning intervention is used to:

options.text: Confirm the intervention changed the relevant test marker
Replace the need for clinical reasoning

explanation: Retesting checks whether the chosen intervention changed the expected test marker. This is the B phase of the A-B-A design. If the marker changes, the intervention is working. If not, re-evaluate technique or pattern identification.

stem: In the B PEC case study, visit 1 findings are: +B [[ADT]], +B [[PADT]], −B [[PART]], [[SRT]] 8", Squat 1/5, [[HALT]] 1/1. The treatment is:

options.text: B PEC repositioning (Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR w/ Balloon, Stair Short Seated Balloon)

explanation: Visit 1 with all tests positive = repositioning phase. <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> uses Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular Internal Rotation">FA IR</abbr> with Balloon, and Stair Short Seated Balloon. A uses different exercises. C is premature. D applies only after tests clear.

stem: In the B PEC case study, visit 2 shows: −B [[ADT]], +B [[PADT]]. The focus shifts to:

options.text: Mediastinum expansion and abdominals with reciprocal repositioning

explanation: Visit 2: <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> clears but <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists. Emphasize mediastinum expansion and abs with reciprocal repositioning. This targets the respiratory component of the pattern. A is unnecessary (<abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> cleared). C is later. D is premature.

stem: In the L AIC case study, visit 2 shows: −B [[ADT]], −B [[PADT]], +R [[PART]]. The next step is:

options.text: Inhibit left posterior mediastinum, promote right inlet abduction via right glute max

explanation: Visit 2 in <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> case: <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> and <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> clear but R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> persists. Inhibit L posterior mediastinum and promote R inlet abduction via R glute max. C applies only when all tests are negative. B is unnecessary since <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> cleared.

stem: In the B PEC case study, visit 5 shows: −B [[PADT]], −B [[PART]], [[HALT]] 4/4. The next goal is:

options.text: Integrate right outlet adduction via right inferior glute max

explanation: Visit 5: <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> and <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> both negative, <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 4/4. The next step is integrating R outlet adduction via R inferior glute max. This corresponds to advancing from <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 4 toward <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 5. D is premature — integration work remains.

stem: In the B PEC case study, visit 6 shows: [[HALT]] 5/5, Squat 4/5. The program transitions to:

options.text: Reciprocal/alternating function (retro walking, heel stair descents, reverse squatting)

explanation: Visit 6: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 5/5 and Squat 4/5 = integration into reciprocal/alternating function. Activities include retro walking, heel stair descents, and reverse squatting. The patient has progressed through the full hierarchy.

stem: In the [[HALT]], what does the patient do at Step 1?

options.text: Press bottom toes into the wall to stabilize the pelvis
Raise the top leg off the wall
Turn the top heel up and top toes down

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Step 1 = press bottom toes into wall (stabilize pelvis). Step 2 = press bottom hip into surface. Step 3 = turn/raise bottom knee. Step 4 = turn top heel up. Step 5 = raise top leg and extend hip. B = Step 5. C = Step 4. D = Step 5.

stem: In the [[HALT]], Step 3 requires the patient to:

options.text: Press bottom hip into the surface
Turn/raise bottom knee using IC adductor and anterior glute med while top hip stays
Raise top leg off wall and extend hip

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Step 3 = turn/raise bottom knee using IC adductor and anterior glute med (top hip stays). This step tests the ability to achieve outlet abduction of the flexed leg. A = Step 2. C = Step 5. D = Step 1.

stem: During the [[PART]], the examiner should stop the test if:

options.text: The patient can't maintain knee extension

explanation: During <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, the examiner passively abducts the top leg while stabilizing the innominate. Stop if thoracic sidebending occurs — this indicates compensation rather than true hip abduction. Positive = abduction < 45°.

stem: What constitutes a positive [[PART]]?

options.text: Top knee can't reach the mat

explanation: Positive <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> = top leg abduction < 45°. A is a negative (normal) finding. C describes positive <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>. D describes positive <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>.

stem: The Apical Expansion Test setup is:

options.text: Supine, knees flexed, lumbar flat. On exhalation, guide one side's ribs down. Hold ribs, patient inhales into opposite chest wall. Compare sides.
Standing, arms overhead. Examiner palpates lateral ribcage during forced exhalation.
Sidelying, hips/knees 90°. Examiner palpates posterior mediastinum during inhalation.
Prone, arms at sides. Examiner observes rib motion during tidal breathing.

explanation: Apical Expansion Test: patient supine, knees flexed, lumbar flat. On exhalation, guide one side's ribs down. Hold ribs down and have the patient inhale into the opposite chest wall. Compare sides. C describes the Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test.

stem: The Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test setup is:

options.text: Supine, knees flexed. Examiner guides ribs down on one side.
Patient on side, hips/knees 90°, back rounded. One hand on posterior mediastinum, one on lateral/anterior ribs. Assess expansion on inhalation.
Standing, forward bend. Examiner palpates paraspinals.
Prone, arms at sides. Examiner observes posterior rib expansion.

explanation: Posterior Outlet Mediastinum Expansion Test: patient on side, hips/knees 90°, back rounded. One hand on posterior mediastinum, one on lateral/anterior ribs. Assess expansion on inhalation. Repeat on the other side. A describes the Apical Expansion Test.

stem: During the 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive FA IR with Balloon, the patient's feet should be:

options.text: Together on the wall, toes pointed
Wider than the hips on the wall with a ball between the knees

explanation: The patient lies supine with feet flat on the wall, hips and knees at 90°. Move feet out wider than the hips. Place a 4–6 inch ball between the knees. The wider foot position with ball squeeze facilitates passive <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular Internal Rotation">FA IR</abbr> during the breathing sequence.

stem: In the 90-90 Hip Lift with Balloon, what does the patient do on the exhale?

options.text: Inhale through the nose and relax
Blow into the balloon while maintaining a pelvic tilt, then pause 3 seconds with tongue on roof of mouth
Press feet flat into the wall

explanation: On exhale: blow into the balloon while performing a pelvic tilt (tailbone slightly off mat, low back flat). Pause 3 seconds with tongue on roof of mouth to prevent air escape. C is specifically discouraged — dig down with heels instead of pressing feet flat. D opposes the exercise goal.

stem: In the Modified All Four Belly Lift, after maximally rounding the spine and shifting weight forward, the patient should:

options.text: Raise one hand off the floor while maintaining rounded trunk, then breathe 4–5 breaths before switching sides
Extend the spine and lift the head
Shift weight backward toward the heels

explanation: After rounding the spine and shifting weight forward (nose over fingertips), raise one hand while maintaining the rounded trunk without twisting. The contralateral abdominal wall engages. Hold for 4–5 breaths, then switch. B opposes the exercise. C reduces abdominal demand.

stem: In the Standing Wall Supported Reach, what should the patient's lower back be doing?

options.text: Arched away from the wall (lordosis maintained)
Flat on the wall throughout — upper back comes off wall during reach, lower back stays
Completely off the wall at all times

explanation: The lower back (mid-back and down) stays flat on the wall. On exhale, the patient reaches arms forward/down so the upper back comes off the wall while the lower back remains flat. On the next inhale, the patient expands the upper back. A defeats the purpose. C removes the wall feedback.

stem: In the Right Sidelying Respiratory Left Adductor Pull Back, the left knee position relative to the left hip and ankle should be:

options.text: Left knee should be higher than the left hip
Left knee should be lower than the left hip and ankle
Left knee should be at the same height as the ankle
Left knee should be fully extended

explanation: A bolster is placed between the feet and a towel between the knees. The left knee should be lower than the left hip and ankle. This positioning biases the left hip toward internal rotation and adduction, facilitating left <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> when the patient pulls back and squeezes down.

stem: The technique description notation "Letters preceding / = Position; Letters following / = Action" means:

options.text: The slash separates the starting position from the movement the patient performs during the exercise
The slash separates left from right
The slash separates inlet from outlet
The slash separates breathing phase from movement

explanation: In PRI technique descriptions, letters before the slash describe the position the patient is placed in; letters after describe the action they perform. When no slash exists, the notation describes an action. This convention appears in the manual's terminology key.

stem: [[HALT]] Level 4 tests the integration between:

options.text: IC adductor and anterior glute med
Contralateral hip adductors and ipsilateral hip abductor (gluteus medius)
Glute max extension during concomitant abduction

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 4 = ability to raise the top leg completely off the wall and hold without using lateral trunk muscle. Inability reflects poor integration between contralateral hip adductors and ipsilateral hip abductor (gluteus medius). A = Level 1. B = Level 2. D = Level 5.

stem: [[HALT]] Level 5 requires the patient to:

options.text: Press bottom toes into the wall
Move the correctly abducted top leg into extension without extending the low back, flexing the knee, or rotating the leg externally
Hold the sidelying position for 30 seconds

explanation: <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> Level 5: move the correctly abducted top lower extremity into extension without extending the low back, flexing the knee, or rotating the leg externally (<abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular External Rotation">FA ER</abbr>). Inability = can't extend with glute max during concomitant abduction. A = Step 1. B = Step 3.

stem: In L AIC, the left respiratory findings include:

options.text: Left anterior ribs flared/ER, left diaphragm descended/linear/postural (poor ZOA), left IO/TAs weak, left anterior thorax hyperinflated
Bilateral anterior ribs flared, bilateral diaphragms descended
Left posterior mediastinum expanded, right posterior mediastinum limited

explanation: <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> respiratory findings: left anterior ribs flared/<abbr title="External Rotation">ER</abbr>, left diaphragm descended/linear/postural (poor <abbr title="Zone of Apposition">ZOA</abbr>), left <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs weak, left anterior thorax hyperinflated, left posterior mediastinum limited. B = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> findings. D reverses the mediastinum findings.

stem: PRI uses the term "pelvic diaphragm in a state of inhalation" to describe:

options.text: The pelvic floor literally breathing in air
A chronically descended pelvic floor that has lost its ability to cycle between descent and ascension
Active upward movement of the pelvic diaphragm

explanation: The metaphor means: just as a flattened respiratory diaphragm reflects a stuck-inhale thorax, a descended pelvic floor reflects a stuck-inhale pelvis. The pelvic floor doesn't literally inhale. During actual inhalation, the pelvic floor eccentrically descends to accommodate increased <abbr title="Intra-Abdominal Pressure">IAP</abbr>. PRI's point is that a chronically descended pelvic floor has lost its cycling ability.

stem: Strong pelvic diaphragm contraction is most efficiently coupled with which respiratory phase?

options.text: Mid and end phase of exhalation

explanation: Strong pelvic diaphragm contraction produces more efficient abdominal co-contraction, most efficient during mid and end phase of exhalation. <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr> and <abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> contractions are closely coupled with pelvic diaphragm activity. Pelvic diaphragm strength correlates with increased FEV, flow, and FVC.

stem: Dyssynchronous pelvic floor patterns are associated with:

options.text: Decreased IAP, relaxed abdominals, improved respiratory mechanics
Increased IAP (Valsalva, abdominal bracing), global activation, external oblique dominance
Improved diaphragmatic excursion and pelvic floor ascension
Isolated IO/TA activation with efficient breathing

explanation: Dyssynchronous patterns: pelvic diaphragm descension associated with increased <abbr title="Intra-Abdominal Pressure">IAP</abbr> (Valsalva, abdominal bracing) and global activation. External obliques are predominantly recruited during abdominal bracing. Overuse of EOs and rectus abdominis is noted in nulliparous women with SUI.

stem: Decreased diaphragmatic motion combined with increased pelvic diaphragm descent and increased respiratory rate is associated with:

options.text: Patients with painful Active Straight Leg Raise ([[ASLR]])

explanation: Research shows decreased diaphragmatic motion, increased pelvic diaphragm descent, increased minute ventilation and respiratory rates in patients with painful ASLR. This reflects the dyssynchronous respiratory-pelvic relationship seen in PRI patterns.

stem: Voluntary abdominal activity has what effect on the pelvic diaphragm?

options.text: Has no effect on pelvic diaphragm activity

explanation: Research shows that voluntary abdominal activity increases pelvic diaphragm muscle activity. <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr> and <abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> contractions are closely coupled with pelvic diaphragm activity. This is why PRI exercises integrate abdominal activation with pelvic floor restoration.

stem: If the right inlet is in R IP ER, what is the right AF position?
explanation: Inlet direction matches <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr> direction. R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> = R <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral External Rotation">AF ER</abbr>. A is the inverse. C is the wrong side. D describes femoral-on-acetabular motion, not acetabular-on-femoral.

stem: R SI IR describes:

options.text: Right sacrum rotating inward on the right ilium at the posterior outlet — outlet closing
Right ilium rotating inward on the sacrum at the posterior inlet
Right acetabulum rotating on the femur

explanation: <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> = Sacro-Ilio = sacrum rotating on the ilium at the posterior outlet. R <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac Internal Rotation">SI IR</abbr> = right sacrum rotating inward on the right ilium = posterior outlet closing. B describes <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr> (posterior inlet). C describes <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr>. D describes <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral">AF</abbr>.

stem: What is the relationship between L IsP IR and L IP ER?

options.text: They are identical — same position described differently
They are in the same equivalence chain — inlet opens while outlet simultaneously closes
They are opposites — one is corrected and the other is patterned
They describe different sides of the pelvis

explanation: L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> (inlet opens) and L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr> (outlet closes) are in the same equivalence chain (L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IsP IR</abbr> = L <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac Internal Rotation">SI IR</abbr> = L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral External Rotation">AF ER</abbr>). The inlet and outlet move reciprocally — when one opens, the other closes on the same side.

stem: When [[ADT]] is negative bilaterally and L [[PADT]] is positive, with a positive [[SRT]], the findings are most consistent with:

options.text: L AIC or B PEC pattern (standard)

explanation: When <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> is negative bilaterally (repositioned) but <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists with positive <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr>, this reflects a standard <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> or <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> pattern. <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> would show <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> = 0 (touches toes). C is wrong because <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists. The functional relationship table in the manual maps −L <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>, +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, +<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> to standard patterns.

stem: When [[ADT]] is negative bilaterally, [[PADT]] is negative bilaterally, but R [[PART]] is positive, the next facilitation target is:
explanation: −L <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>, −L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>, +R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> = Myokinematic territory or late-stage Pelvis Restoration. The R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> persists because R glute max facilitation is needed.
