The cause has shifted from pelvic malalignment to R proximal iliacus overactivity blocking R glute max.

stem: The functional relationship chain shows: −L [[ADT]], +L [[PADT]], −[[SRT]]. What does the negative [[SRT]] suggest?

options.text: Standard L AIC or B PEC pattern
Patho PEC — long and tonic muscles, everything "OFF"

explanation: When <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> is negative but <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists and <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> is negative (0" = touches toes), this pattern suggests Patho <abbr title="Posterior Exterior Chain">PEC</abbr> with long and tonic muscles — everything is "OFF" in the pelvic inlet and outlet. A would show <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> > 0".

stem: The left gluteus medius belongs to which color family?
explanation: Left gluteus medius = Orange family (Transverse Plane). It acts at the inlet, producing left <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> (counter-nutation) in closed chain. A = L iliacus. B = L obturator internus and L iliococcygeus. D = R glute max, R piriformis, R coccygeus.

stem: Which muscles belong to the Violet (Internal Rotation) family?

options.text: R gluteus maximus (inlet & outlet), R piriformis (outlet), R coccygeus (outlet)

explanation: Violet (<abbr title="Internal Rotation">IR</abbr>) = R gluteus maximus (inlet & outlet), R piriformis (outlet), R coccygeus (outlet). These produce right-sided internal rotation that opposes the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> pattern. A = Green. C = Orange. D = Yellow.

stem: The iliococcygeus belongs to the pelvic outlet. What is its PRI action?

options.text: Left outlet abduction — pulls the left ischial tuberosity laterally, helping widen the outlet (IsP ER)

explanation: Left iliococcygeus produces left outlet abduction — pulls the left ischial tuberosity laterally, widening the left outlet (<abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr>) and helping ascend the left pelvic diaphragm. Part of levator ani. Green family. Facilitated in Step 2 of <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> hierarchy.

stem: Which posterior inlet muscles are seen on the Pelvic Inlet image from Handout 1?
explanation: The Pelvic Inlet image (posterior view) in Handout 1 shows gluteus medius (1) and gluteus maximus (2). These govern the posterior pelvic inlet. A = anterior inlet muscles (separate image). C and D = outlet muscles.

stem: What is the overall goal of L AIC correction expressed in PRI equivalence terms?

options.text: Move from L AF ER (patterned) to L AF IR (neutral/restored)
Move from L AF IR to L AF ER

explanation: The goal of <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> correction is to move the pelvis from L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral External Rotation">AF ER</abbr> (patterned — inlet open, outlet closed) to L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> (neutral/restored — inlet closed, outlet open). B is moving into the pattern. C = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr>. D = bilateral outlet closing = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> position.

stem: The 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge specifically engages the left hamstring. Why?

options.text: The left hamstring pulls the left hemipelvis posteriorly, opposing the anterior tilt of L AIC
The left hamstring extends the left knee
The left hamstring produces left inlet opening (IP ER)
The left hamstring facilitates left TFL activation

explanation: The left hamstring (outlet muscle) pulls the left hemipelvis posteriorly, opposing the anterior tilt of the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> pattern. With the pelvic tilt maintained and right leg straight, the left leg on the wall allows the left hamstring to drive posterior pelvic rotation. B is open-chain action. C is the patterned direction.

stem: In the 90-90 Supported Hip Lift with Hemibridge, the patient is instructed to "dig down with your heels" rather than press feet flat into the wall. Why?

options.text: Pressing flat activates the quadriceps; digging with heels preferentially activates the hamstrings
It prevents the feet from sliding

explanation: Digging down with the heels biases hamstring activation over quadriceps. The manual explicitly says "Do not press your feet flat into the wall instead dig down with your heels." The goal is posterior pelvic pull via hamstrings, not quad-driven wall pressing.

stem: Step 5 of the L AIC hierarchy requires [[HALT]] at what level?
explanation: Step 5 = upright left single-leg frontal plane static and dynamic control. Requires <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 3/5. The patient must demonstrate adequate pelvic control before transitioning to upright single-leg work. Earlier steps correspond to lower <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> levels (Step 1 = 0/5, Step 2 = 1/5, Step 3 = 2/5, Step 4 = 4/5).

stem: Which exercise is used for Left Anterior Inlet Inhibition to stop the TFL from pulling the pelvis into anterior tilt?
explanation: The 90-90 Respiratory Left Hip Shift is used specifically for Left Anterior Inlet Inhibition to stop the <abbr title="Tensor Fasciae Latae">TFL</abbr> from pulling the pelvis into anterior tilt. A is a repositioning exercise. C is for bilateral inhibition. D is for right glute max facilitation.

stem: The corrected left outlet position (L IsP ER) is achieved by facilitating which muscles?

options.text: Left pelvic floor muscles — obturator internus, iliococcygeus, puborectalis, pubococcygeus (via left adductor activation)
Right glute max and right piriformis
Left rectus femoris and left sartorius

explanation: L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> (outlet opening) is facilitated by left pelvic floor muscles accessed via left adductor activation. The adductors create femoral adduction which, in closed chain, activates the pelvic floor to pull ischial tuberosities apart (outlet abduction = <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr>). This is facilitation Step 2.

stem: Step 9 of the L AIC hierarchy includes which activity?

options.text: Left Stance Interrupted Reciprocal Step Through or Two Point Gait with Right Glute Max

explanation: Step 9 = reciprocal alternating activity. Includes Left Stance Interrupted Reciprocal Step Through and Two Point Gait with Right Glute Max to integrate the corrected position into walking. A = repositioning. C = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> repositioning. D = <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> repositioning.

stem: PRI's claim that the diaphragm's crura "pull the dorsal spine up and forward" is:

options.text: Anatomically precise — the crura lift the thoracic spine anteriorly
Functionally correct but anatomically imprecise — the crura increase lumbar lordosis via L1–L3, not truly pulling the dorsal spine up
Completely incorrect — the crura have no spinal attachment
Only true on the left side

explanation: The crura attach to anterior surfaces of L1–L3. Crural pull increases lumbar lordosis and anterior spinal orientation, not truly pulling the dorsal spine up. PRI conflates crural and costal diaphragm actions. The costal portion elevates lower ribs; the crural portion tensions the lumbar spine. The net effect description is functionally correct but anatomically imprecise.

stem: In B Patho PEC, the rectus abdominis is:
explanation: In <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr>, the rectus abdominis is eccentrically tight/strained. This is in addition to the bilateral <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/TAs being long/weak with abdominal AND pelvic bulge. The eccentric strain of the rectus reflects the long and tonic muscle state characteristic of Patho.

stem: What does AIC stand for?
explanation: <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr> = Anterior Interior Chain. The chain consists of diaphragm → psoas → iliacus → <abbr title="Tensor Fasciae Latae">TFL</abbr> → vastus lateralis → biceps femoris. "Left <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr>" = the left side's anterior interior chain is dominant/patterned.

stem: What does PEC stand for?
explanation: <abbr title="Posterior Exterior Chain">PEC</abbr> = Posterior Exterior Chain. <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> = Bilateral Posterior Exterior Chain = bilateral extension dominance. A invents a wrong expansion. C and D are not PRI terms.

stem: What does BC stand for in PRI?
explanation: BC = Brachial Chain — the upper quadrant chain. Note: BC details are largely out of scope for the Pelvis Restoration course beyond defining the acronym.

stem: The [[PADT]] is most associated with assessing which pelvic structure's function?

options.text: Femoral head seating in the acetabulum

explanation: The <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> assesses pelvic diaphragm function — specifically the ability of the pelvic floor to ascend. Positive = the pelvic diaphragm on that side cannot ascend because the outlet ischium can't abduct and the inlet ilium can't adduct. B relates more to <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>. C is assessed by <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr> position.

stem: In the L AIC test profile, the asymmetry between [[ADT]]/[[PADT]] (positive left) and [[PART]] (positive right) is explained by:

options.text: The left inlet is stuck in IP ER (blocks left femoral adduction) while the right compensatory position (R IP IR) blocks right femoral abduction
The examiner is performing the tests incorrectly
PART always tests the opposite side from ADT by convention

explanation: The left inlet is stuck in <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo External Rotation">IP ER</abbr>, which blocks left femoral adduction (→ +L <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>). The right side compensates in R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> (closed inlet), which causes lateral buttressing of the right femoral head against the superior acetabulum, blocking right hip abduction (→ +R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>). The asymmetry reflects the asymmetric <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> pattern.

stem: Which pattern uses asymmetric (left-specific) repositioning techniques?

options.text: B PEC and B Patho PEC share the same repositioning with L AIC

explanation: <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> uses asymmetric/left-specific repositioning: Hemibridge, R Sidelying L Adductor Pull Back, L Sidelying R Glute Max. <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> and <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> use the SAME three bilateral/symmetrical repositioning techniques (Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift). D is incorrect — the sets are different.

stem: B PEC and B Patho PEC share the same repositioning techniques. True or false, and why?

options.text: True — both are bilateral extension patterns requiring the same initial repositioning approach
False — Patho requires completely different exercises
True — but only because Patho doesn't exist as a separate category

explanation: True. Both <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> and <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> use the same three repositioning techniques: Standing Wall Supported Reach, 90-90 Hip Lift in Passive <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular Internal Rotation">FA IR</abbr> with Balloon, Modified All Four Belly Lift. Both are bilateral extension patterns. The difference is in subsequent treatment hierarchy — Patho may need straw/kazoo instead of balloon.

stem: The technique description "Standing Wall Supported Respiratory Left AF IR with Left IO/TA Integration" has no slash (/). This means:

options.text: All terms describe the action (when no slash exists, the notation describes action)
It describes both position and action simultaneously

explanation: Per the manual's terminology key: letters preceding / = position, letters following / = action. When no / exists, the entire description = action. So "Standing Wall Supported Respiratory Left <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr> with Left <abbr title="Internal Oblique">IO</abbr>/<abbr title="Transversus Abdominis">TA</abbr> Integration" describes what the patient actively does.

stem: PRI uses "Patho," "Pathologic," and "Pathological" in the manual. These:

options.text: All mean the same thing — PRI's editing is inconsistent
Are used in different PRI courses

explanation: "Patho," "Pathologic," and "Pathological" all appear in the manual and mean the same thing. PRI's editing is inconsistent. Similarly, the course title uses "Pelvis" as an adjective ("Pelvis Restoration") rather than the standard "Pelvic Restoration."

stem: After the B PEC case study visit 4 shows −B [[PADT]], −L [[PART]], +R [[PART]], the next target is:

options.text: Promote right inlet abduction via right posterior glute max

explanation: Visit 4 in the <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> case: <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> clears bilaterally, L <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> negative but R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> positive. The right side needs glute max facilitation to achieve right inlet abduction. C was visit 3's target. B is unnecessary since <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr>/<abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> cleared.

stem: Which anterior inlet muscles are labeled in Handout 1's Pelvic Inlet illustration?

options.text: Iliacus (1), Sartorius (2), Rectus Femoris (3), IO and TA (4)

explanation: The anterior Pelvic Inlet illustration labels: 1. Iliacus, 2. Sartorius, 3. Rectus Femoris, 4. Internal Oblique and Transverse Abdominis. A is the posterior inlet image. C and D show outlet muscles.

stem: What does IsP stand for?
explanation: <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo">IsP</abbr> = Ischio-Pubo = the anterior pelvic outlet. Bordered by the ischial tuberosities and pubic symphysis. A conflates <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral">IS</abbr> and P. C and D are not PRI terms.

stem: In the [[PADT]], the patient is sidelying with both hips and knees at 90°. What makes this test "active" rather than "passive"?

options.text: The patient actively brings the top knee toward the mat
Both the patient and examiner move the limb together

explanation: The <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> is an active test — the patient does the work. They actively bring the top leg back to neutral (knee toward mat) while maintaining 90° knee flexion. Contrast with <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr>, which is passive — the examiner moves the limb.

stem: The [[PART]] interpretation changes depending on [[ADT]] status. When [[ADT]] is positive on the left, a positive right [[PART]] reflects:

options.text: Lateral buttressing of the right femoral head against the superior acetabulum from the compensatory pelvic position
Right proximal iliacus overactivity blocking right glute max

explanation: When <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> is positive (pelvis still malaligned), the positive R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> is caused by the compensatory R <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> position creating lateral buttressing of the right femoral head. B applies when <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> becomes negative. C is not the PRI interpretation. D describes a different finding.

stem: The muscles inhibited during L AIC correction include:

options.text: Left TFL, left psoas major, right adductor magnus, right hamstrings
Left glute max, left glute med, left IO/TAs
Right rectus femoris, right sartorius, left obturator internus

explanation: <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> inhibition targets the overactive pattern muscles: left <abbr title="Tensor Fasciae Latae">TFL</abbr>, left psoas major, right adductor magnus, and right hamstrings. B lists corrective muscles that should be facilitated, not inhibited. C lists Step 1 facilitation muscles.

stem: In the inverse equivalence chain, L IP IR = L IS ER. What does L IS ER describe mechanically?

options.text: Counter-nutation — ilium rotating outward on sacrum, decompressing the SI joint

explanation: L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = counter-nutation = ilium rotating outward on the sacrum. This decompresses the <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> joint (opposite of nutation). In the corrected chain: L <abbr title="Ilio-Pubo Internal Rotation">IP IR</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac External Rotation">SI ER</abbr> = L <abbr title="Acetabulo-Femoral Internal Rotation">AF IR</abbr>. A describes <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr> (nutation). C = <abbr title="Ischio-Pubo External Rotation">IsP ER</abbr> (correct but describes a different joint). D = <abbr title="Femoro-Acetabular External Rotation">FA ER</abbr>.

stem: Why can't you skip repositioning and go directly to facilitation?

options.text: Facilitation exercises are too advanced for beginners
Facilitation targets muscles that can't fire correctly from a malaligned position
Repositioning is always faster than facilitation

explanation: Skipping repositioning means facilitation targets muscles that can't fire correctly from a malaligned position. The pelvis must first be moved out of its patterned position before underactive muscles can be effectively activated. This is why the sequence is Reposition → Inhibit → Facilitate.

stem: Step 10 of the L AIC hierarchy promotes:
explanation: Step 10 = squatting. This is the final step of the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> hierarchy, integrating full pelvic control into a deep squat movement. Steps 5–10 progress from single-leg control through gait to squatting.

stem: The Karimi et al. 2021 RCT (n=18) on pelvic repositioning exercises showed:

options.text: Significant improvements, but the sample size is very small and should not be over-weighted
Strong evidence conclusively proving PRI's framework

explanation: Karimi et al. 2021 (n=18) showed significant improvements from pelvic repositioning exercises. However, n=18 is very small. PRI's own EBP document argues A-B-A designs are more appropriate than RCTs, yet PRI cites this RCT when it supports them. Legitimate preliminary evidence that should not be over-weighted.

stem: For the B PEC version of the [[SRT]], how does the patient perform the reach?

options.text: Reach toward left toes with left hip shift back, then repeat toward right
Reach straight down toward the floor, hips move back symmetrically

explanation: <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> (no hip shift): patient reaches straight down toward the floor with hips moving back symmetrically. Measure from fingertips to floor. A is the <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> version with hip shift. C and D are not standard <abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> procedures.

stem: A mnemonic for remembering B Patho PEC distinguishing features is "Patho = Paradoxical performance." This means:

options.text: Better ROM/squat scores despite positive ADT/PADT — better performance = worse pathology
Worse ROM and worse squat scores
Normal test findings with severe symptoms
Alternating positive and negative tests from visit to visit

explanation: Patho = Paradoxical performance. The patient touches toes (<abbr title="Standing Reach Test">SRT</abbr> = 0") and squats well (> 3/5) despite having positive bilateral <abbr title="Adduction Drop Test">ADT</abbr> and <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr>. Better ROM = worse pathology. This is compensatory hypermobility from long and tonic muscles, not motor control.

stem: The sacrotuberous ligament is taut on which side in L AIC?
explanation: In <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr>, the left sacrum is nutated (L <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral Internal Rotation">IS IR</abbr>) — the left <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> is compressed and the sacrotuberous ligament is taut on the left. The right <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> is counter-nutated (R <abbr title="Ilio-Sacral External Rotation">IS ER</abbr>) — sacrotuberous and sacrospinous ligaments are lax on the right, contributing to right <abbr title="Sacro-Iliac">SI</abbr> instability.

stem: The B Patho PEC hierarchy includes PRI Manual Brachial Chain and Pelvic Floor techniques in Step 1. These are from:

options.text: The Postural Respiration course (out of scope)

explanation: The Brachial Chain techniques referenced in the <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> hierarchy are from the Postural Respiration course, which is out of scope for the Pelvis Restoration exam. The mention should be noted as "(out of scope)" in study materials.

stem: In the Left Sidelying Right Glute Max exercise, the patient first shifts the right hip forward until feeling:

options.text: A slight stretch or pull in the left outside hip (buttock)

explanation: The patient shifts the right hip forward until feeling a slight stretch in the left outside hip (buttock). Then turns the right knee out using the wall as a fulcrum. They should feel the right outside hip (glute max) engage without moving the right hip back.

stem: Which hierarchy step is associated with R HALT 2/5?
explanation: Step 3 test markers: +L <abbr title="Pelvic Ascension Drop Test">PADT</abbr> persists, −R <abbr title="Passive Abduction Raise Test">PART</abbr> achieved, R <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 2/5. The <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> level maps to the facilitation step: Step 1 = <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 0/5, Step 2 = <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 1/5, Step 3 = <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 2/5, Step 4 = <abbr title="Hruska Abduction Lift Test">HALT</abbr> 4/5.

stem: The third step of both B PEC and B Patho PEC hierarchies is:

options.text: Maximize inhibition of bilateral anterior inlet and posterior outlet
Left AIC Pattern Pelvis Restoration Facilitation (Alternating) Program

explanation: Both <abbr title="Bilateral PEC">B PEC</abbr> and <abbr title="Bilateral Pathological PEC">B Patho PEC</abbr> hierarchies: Step 1 = maximize diaphragmatic respiratory function, Step 2 = maximize bilateral inlet/outlet inhibition, Step 3 = Left <abbr title="Anterior Interior Chain">AIC</abbr> Pattern Pelvis Restoration Facilitation (Alternating) Program. The bilateral patterns eventually funnel into <abbr title="Left Anterior Interior Chain">L AIC</abbr> alternating work.
